solution:
Yes, there must exist infinitely many such $n$.
Let $S$ be the convex hull of the set of points $(n,
a_n)$ for $n \geq 0$. Geometrically, $S$ is the intersection of
all convex sets (or even all halfplanes) containing the points
$(n, a_n)$; algebraically, $S$ is the set of points $(x,y)$
which can be written as $c_1(n_1, a_{n_1}) + \cdots + c_k(n_k, a_{n_k})$
for some $c_1, \dots, c_k$ which are nonnegative of sum 1.

We prove that for infinitely many $n$, $(n, a_n)$ is a vertex on the upper
boundary of $S$, and that these $n$ satisfy the given
condition. The condition that $(n, a_n)$ is a vertex on the upper
boundary of $S$ is equivalent to the existence of a line passing through
$(n, a_n)$ with all other points of $S$ below it.
That is, there should exist $m>0$ such that
\begin{equation} \label{eq1}
a_k < a_n + m(k-n) \qquad \forall k \geq 1.
\end{equation}

We first show that $n=1$ satisfies (\ref{eq1}). The condition
$a_k/k \to 0$ as $k \to \infty$
implies that $(a_k - a_1)/(k-1) \to 0$ as well. Thus the
set $\{(a_k-a_1)/(k-1)\}$ has an upper bound $m$, and now
$a_k \leq a_1 + m(k-1)$, as desired.

Next, we show that given one $n$ satisfying (\ref{eq1}), there exists a
larger one also satisfying (\ref{eq1}). Again, the condition
$a_k/k \to 0$ as $k \to \infty$ implies that $(a_k-a_n)/(k-n) \to 0$ as
$k \to \infty$. Thus the sequence $\{(a_k-a_n)/(k-n)\}_{k>n}$ has a
maximum element; suppose $k = r$ is the largest value that
achieves this maximum, and put
$m = (a_r -a_n)/(r-n)$. Then the line through
$(r, a_r)$ of slope $m$ lies strictly above $(k, a_k)$ for $k > r$
and passes through or lies above $(k, a_k)$ for $k< r$.
Thus (\ref{eq1})
holds for $n=r$ with $m$ replaced by $m-\epsilon$ for suitably small
$\epsilon > 0$.

By induction, we have that (\ref{eq1}) holds for infinitely many $n$.
For any such $n$ there exists $m>0$ such that for $i=1, \dots, n-1$, the
points $(n-i, a_{n-i})$ and $(n+i, a_{n+i})$ lie below the line through
$(n, a_n)$ of slope $m$. That means $a_{n+i} < a_n + mi$
and $a_{n-i} < a_n - mi$; adding these together gives
$a_{n-i} + a_{n+i} < 2a_n$, as desired.

\end{itemize}

\end{document}

descriptive_long.question:
Assume that $(seqelem)_{indexvar\geq 1}$ is an increasing sequence of
positive real numbers such that
$\lim seqelem/indexvar=0$.  Must there exist infinitely many positive integers
$indexvar$ such that $elemminus+elemplus<2\,seqelem$ for $shiftix=1,2,\ldots,indexvar-1$?
descriptive_long.solution:
Yes, there must exist infinitely many such $indexvar$.
Let $convexhull$ be the convex hull of the set of points $(indexvar,
seqelem)$ for $indexvar \geq 0$. Geometrically, $convexhull$ is the intersection of
all convex sets (or even all halfplanes) containing the points
$(indexvar, seqelem)$; algebraically, $convexhull$ is the set of points $(coordx,coordy)$
which can be written as $coeffone(indexone, a_{indexone}) + \cdots + coeffgen(n_k, a_{n_k})$
for some $coeffone, \dots, coeffgen$ which are nonnegative of sum 1.

We prove that for infinitely many $indexvar$, $(indexvar, seqelem)$ is a vertex on the upper
boundary of $convexhull$, and that these $indexvar$ satisfy the given
condition. The condition that $(indexvar, seqelem)$ is a vertex on the upper
boundary of $convexhull$ is equivalent to the existence of a line passing through
$(indexvar, seqelem)$ with all other points of $convexhull$ below it.
That is, there should exist $slopeval>0$ such that
\begin{equation} \label{eq1}
elemfork < seqelem + slopeval(genericindex-indexvar) \qquad \forall genericindex \geq 1.
\end{equation}

We first show that $indexvar=1$ satisfies (\ref{eq1}). The condition
$elemfork/genericindex \to 0$ as $genericindex \to \infty$
implies that $(elemfork - firstelem)/(genericindex-1) \to 0$ as well. Thus the
set $\{(elemfork-firstelem)/(genericindex-1)\}$ has an upper bound $slopeval$, and now
$elemfork \leq firstelem + slopeval(genericindex-1)$, as desired.

Next, we show that given one $indexvar$ satisfying (\ref{eq1}), there exists a
larger one also satisfying (\ref{eq1}). Again, the condition
$elemfork/genericindex \to 0$ as $genericindex \to \infty$ implies that $(elemfork-seqelem)/(genericindex-indexvar) \to 0$ as
$genericindex \to \infty$. Thus the sequence $\{(elemfork-seqelem)/(genericindex-indexvar)\}_{genericindex>indexvar}$ has a
maximum element; suppose $genericindex = maxindex$ is the largest value that
achieves this maximum, and put
$slopeval = (elemforr -seqelem)/(maxindex-indexvar)$. Then the line through
$(maxindex, elemforr)$ of slope $slopeval$ lies strictly above $(genericindex, elemfork)$ for $genericindex > maxindex$
and passes through or lies above $(genericindex, elemfork)$ for $genericindex< maxindex$.
Thus (\ref{eq1})
holds for $indexvar=maxindex$ with $slopeval$ replaced by $slopeval - smallerror$ for suitably small
$smallerror > 0$.

By induction, we have that (\ref{eq1}) holds for infinitely many $indexvar$.
For any such $indexvar$ there exists $slopeval>0$ such that for $shiftix=1, \dots, indexvar-1$, the
points $(indexvar-shiftix, elemminus)$ and $(indexvar+shiftix, elemplus)$ lie below the line through
$(indexvar, seqelem)$ of slope $slopeval$. That means $elemplus < seqelem + slopeval\,shiftix$
and $elemminus < seqelem - slopeval\,shiftix$; adding these together gives
$elemminus + elemplus < 2seqelem$, as desired.

descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Yes, there must exist infinitely many such $riverbend$.
Let $willowtree$ be the convex hull of the set of points $(riverbend,
sandcastle)$ for $riverbend \geq 0$. Geometrically, $willowtree$ is the intersection of
all convex sets (or even all halfplanes) containing the points
$(riverbend, sandcastle)$; algebraically, $willowtree$ is the set of points $(dreamcatch,stargazer)$
which can be written as $fireflyes(marshmallow, a_{n_1}) + \cdots + thunderclap(n_k, a_{n_k})$
for some $fireflyes, \dots, thunderclap$ which are nonnegative of sum 1.

We prove that for infinitely many $riverbend$, $(riverbend, sandcastle)$ is a vertex on the upper
boundary of $willowtree$, and that these $riverbend$ satisfy the given
condition. The condition that $(riverbend, sandcastle)$ is a vertex on the upper
boundary of $willowtree$ is equivalent to the existence of a line passing through
$(riverbend, sandcastle)$ with all other points of $willowtree$ below it.
That is, there should exist $cloudburst>0$ such that
\begin{equation} \label{eq1}
waterfall < sandcastle + cloudburst(moonstone-riverbend) \qquad \forall moonstone \geq 1.
\end{equation}

We first show that $riverbend=1$ satisfies (\ref{eq1}). The condition
$waterfall/moonstone \to 0$ as $moonstone \to \infty$
implies that $(waterfall - snowflower)/(moonstone-1) \to 0$ as well. Thus the
set $\{(waterfall-snowflower)/(moonstone-1)\}$ has an upper bound $cloudburst$, and now
$waterfall \leq snowflower + cloudburst(moonstone-1)$, as desired.

Next, we show that given one $riverbend$ satisfying (\ref{eq1}), there exists a
larger one also satisfying (\ref{eq1}). Again, the condition
$waterfall/moonstone \to 0$ as $moonstone \to \infty$ implies that $(waterfall-sandcastle)/(moonstone-riverbend) \to 0$ as
$moonstone \to \infty$. Thus the sequence $\{(waterfall-sandcastle)/(moonstone-riverbend)\}_{moonstone>riverbend}$ has a
maximum element; suppose $moonstone = stonehorse$ is the largest value that
achieves this maximum, and put
$cloudburst = (echochamber -sandcastle)/(stonehorse-riverbend)$. Then the line through
$(stonehorse, echochamber)$ of slope $cloudburst$ lies strictly above $(moonstone, waterfall)$ for $moonstone > stonehorse$
and passes through or lies above $(moonstone, waterfall)$ for $moonstone< stonehorse$.
Thus (\ref{eq1})
holds for $riverbend=stonehorse$ with $cloudburst$ replaced by $cloudburst-dawnchorus$ for suitably small
$dawnchorus > 0$.

By induction, we have that (\ref{eq1}) holds for infinitely many $riverbend$.
For any such $riverbend$ there exists $cloudburst>0$ such that for $bookshelf=1, \dots, riverbend-1$, the
points $(riverbend-bookshelf, rainshadow)$ and $(riverbend+bookshelf, driftwood)$ lie below the line through
$(riverbend, sandcastle)$ of slope $cloudburst$. That means $driftwood < sandcastle + cloudburst\,bookshelf$
and $rainshadow < sandcastle - cloudburst\,bookshelf$; adding these together gives
$rainshadow + driftwood < 2sandcastle$, as desired.

\end{itemize}

\end{document}

descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Yes, there must exist infinitely many such $unbounded$.
Let $concavegap$ be the convex hull of the set of points $(unbounded,
decreasingvoid)$ for $unbounded \geq 0$. Geometrically, $concavegap$ is the intersection of
all convex sets (or even all halfplanes) containing the points
$(unbounded, decreasingvoid)$; algebraically, $concavegap$ is the set of points $(verticality,horizontalism)$
which can be written as $variableone(unboundedone, decreasingstart) + \cdots + variablemany(immutable, decreasingelement)$
for some $variableone, \dots, variablemany$ which are nonnegative of sum 1.

We prove that for infinitely many $unbounded$, $(unbounded, decreasingvoid)$ is a vertex on the upper
boundary of $concavegap$, and that these $unbounded$ satisfy the given
condition. The condition that $(unbounded, decreasingvoid)$ is a vertex on the upper
boundary of $concavegap$ is equivalent to the existence of a line passing through
$(unbounded, decreasingvoid)$ with all other points of $concavegap$ below it.
That is, there should exist $flatness>0$ such that
\begin{equation} \label{eq1}
decreasingelement < decreasingvoid + flatness(immutable-unbounded) \qquad \forall immutable \geq 1.
\end{equation}

We first show that $unbounded=1$ satisfies (\ref{eq1}). The condition
decreasingelement/immutable \to 0$ as $immutable \to \infty$
implies that $(decreasingelement - decreasingstart)/(immutable-1) \to 0$ as well. Thus the
set $\{(decreasingelement-decreasingstart)/(immutable-1)\}$ has an upper bound $flatness$, and now
decreasingelement \leq decreasingstart + flatness(immutable-1)$, as desired.

Next, we show that given one $unbounded$ satisfying (\ref{eq1}), there exists a
larger one also satisfying (\ref{eq1}). Again, the condition
decreasingelement/immutable \to 0$ as $immutable \to \infty$ implies that $(decreasingelement-decreasingvoid)/(immutable-unbounded) \to 0$ as
$immutable \to \infty$. Thus the sequence $\{(decreasingelement-decreasingvoid)/(immutable-unbounded)\}_{immutable>unbounded}$ has a
maximum element; suppose $immutable = smallest$ is the largest value that
achieves this maximum, and put
$flatness = (decreasingpeak -decreasingvoid)/(smallest-unbounded)$. Then the line through
$(smallest, decreasingpeak)$ of slope $flatness$ lies strictly above $(immutable, decreasingelement)$ for $immutable > smallest$
and passes through or lies above $(immutable, decreasingelement)$ for $immutable< smallest$.
Thus (\ref{eq1})
holds for $unbounded=smallest$ with $flatness$ replaced by $flatness-largedelta$ for suitably small
$largedelta > 0$.

By induction, we have that (\ref{eq1}) holds for infinitely many $unbounded$.
For any such $unbounded$ there exists $flatness>0$ such that for $totality=1, \dots, unbounded-1$, the
points $(unbounded-totality, decreasingvoid)$ and $(unbounded+totality, decreasingvoid)$ lie below the line through
$(unbounded, decreasingvoid)$ of slope $flatness$. That means $decreasingvoid < decreasingvoid + flatness totality$
and $decreasingvoid < decreasingvoid - flatness totality$; adding these together gives
$leftvacuum + rightvacuum < 2decreasingvoid$, as desired.

\end{itemize}
\end{document}

garbled_string.question:
Assume that $(qzxwvtnp)_{hjgrksla\geq 1}$ is an increasing sequence of
positive real numbers such that
$\lim qzxwvtnp/hjgrksla=0$.  Must there exist infinitely many positive integers
$hjgrksla$ such that $rtewymqs+uzkpcbva<2qzxwvtnp$ for $bvnkserp=1,2,\ldots,hjgrksla-1$?
garbled_string.solution:
Yes, there must exist infinitely many such $hjgrksla$.
Let $sfyqemlc$ be the convex hull of the set of points $(hjgrksla,
qzxwvtnp)$ for $hjgrksla \geq 0$. Geometrically, $sfyqemlc$ is the intersection of
all convex sets (or even all halfplanes) containing the points
$(hjgrksla, qzxwvtnp)$; algebraically, $sfyqemlc$ is the set of points $(oynfrdwe,jgouvkal)$
which can be written as $ncbpyzwo(kdragpum, a_{kdragpum}) + \cdots + tlvwqzse(n_k, a_{n_k})$
for some $ncbpyzwo, \dots, tlvwqzse$ which are nonnegative of sum 1.

We prove that for infinitely many $hjgrksla$, $(hjgrksla, qzxwvtnp)$ is a vertex on the upper
boundary of $sfyqemlc$, and that these $hjgrksla$ satisfy the given
condition. The condition that $(hjgrksla, qzxwvtnp)$ is a vertex on the upper
boundary of $sfyqemlc$ is equivalent to the existence of a line passing through
$(hjgrksla, qzxwvtnp)$ with all other points of $sfyqemlc$ below it.
That is, there should exist $vznphqtc>0$ such that
\begin{equation} \label{eq1}
ydmlfsqe < qzxwvtnp + vznphqtc(pldjqwne-hjgrksla) \qquad \forall pldjqwne \geq 1.
\end{equation}

We first show that $hjgrksla=1$ satisfies (\ref{eq1}). The condition
$ydmlfsqe/pldjqwne \to 0$ as $pldjqwne \to \infty$
implies that $(ydmlfsqe - hvkzrpnt)/(pldjqwne-1) \to 0$ as well. Thus the
set $\{(ydmlfsqe-hvkzrpnt)/(pldjqwne-1)\}$ has an upper bound $vznphqtc$, and now
$ydmlfsqe \leq hvkzrpnt + vznphqtc(pldjqwne-1)$, as desired.

Next, we show that given one $hjgrksla$ satisfying (\ref{eq1}), there exists a
larger one also satisfying (\ref{eq1}). Again, the condition
$ydmlfsqe/pldjqwne \to 0$ as $pldjqwne \to \infty$ implies that $(ydmlfsqe-qzxwvtnp)/(pldjqwne-hjgrksla) \to 0$ as
$pldjqwne \to \infty$. Thus the sequence $\{(ydmlfsqe-qzxwvtnp)/(pldjqwne-hjgrksla)\}_{pldjqwne>hjgrksla}$ has a
maximum element; suppose $pldjqwne = wqibzhad$ is the largest value that
achieves this maximum, and put
$vznphqtc = (fsetvmob -qzxwvtnp)/(wqibzhad-hjgrksla)$. Then the line through
$(wqibzhad, fsetvmob)$ of slope $vznphqtc$ lies strictly above $(pldjqwne, ydmlfsqe)$ for $pldjqwne > wqibzhad$
and passes through or lies above $(pldjqwne, ydmlfsqe)$ for $pldjqwne< wqibzhad$.
Thus (\ref{eq1})
holds for $hjgrksla=wqibzhad$ with $vznphqtc$ replaced by $vznphqtc-gqrnldcx$ for suitably small
$gqrnldcx > 0$.

By induction, we have that (\ref{eq1}) holds for infinitely many $hjgrksla$.
For any such $hjgrksla$ there exists $vznphqtc>0$ such that for $bvnkserp=1, \dots, hjgrksla-1$, the
points $(hjgrksla-bvnkserp, rtewymqs)$ and $(hjgrksla+bvnkserp, uzkpcbva)$ lie below the line through
$(hjgrksla, qzxwvtnp)$ of slope $vznphqtc$. That means $uzkpcbva < qzxwvtnp + vznphqtc bvnkserp$
and $rtewymqs < qzxwvtnp - vznphqtc bvnkserp$; adding these together gives
$rtewymqs + uzkpcbva < 2qzxwvtnp$, as desired.

kernel_variant.question:
Let $(b_n)_{n\ge 0}$ be a strictly increasing sequence of real numbers satisfying
\[
 \lim_{n\to\infty}\frac{b_n}{n}=0.
\]
Show that there exist infinitely many positive integers $n$ such that the simultaneous `mid-point' inequalities
\[
 b_{n-j}+b_{n+j}<2b_n\qquad(j=1,2,\dots ,n)
\]
are satisfied.
kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout we regard the index $k\,(\ge 0)$ as the point 
P_k:=(k,b_k)\in\mathbb R^2.  A line of positive slope passing through P_n and leaving all other points P_k strictly below it will be called a supporting line at P_n; in that case P_n is referred to as an (upper) vertex.  Our aim is to prove that there are infinitely many vertices; every such vertex $n$ will then immediately deliver the desired inequalities.

Step 1.  P_0 is a vertex.
---------------------------------
Because $b_k/k\to0$, the set $\{(b_k-b_0)/k:k\ge1\}$ is bounded.  Pick a real number $M$ strictly larger than its supremum.  Then $b_k<b_0+Mk$ for every $k\ge1$, so all the points $P_k\;(k\ge1)$ lie strictly below the line $y=b_0+Mx$ through $P_0$.  Hence P_0 is a vertex.

Step 2.  From one vertex we construct a larger one.
--------------------------------------------------
Assume P_n is a vertex and let $\ell_n$ be a supporting line through P_n with slope $m_n>0$.  For $k>n$ define
\[
 \varphi(k):=\frac{b_k-b_n}{k-n}>0.
\]
Because $b_k/k\to0$ we have $\varphi(k)\to0$ as $k\to\infty$, so the finite maximum
\[
 m:=\max_{k>n}\varphi(k)
\]
exists.  Choose the **largest** index $r>n$ for which $\varphi(r)=m$ and set P_r:=(r,b_r).

Claim 2.1   P_r is a vertex.

Proof.  Fix $\varepsilon>0$ with
\[
 0<\varepsilon<\frac{m_n-m}{2}\qquad(\text{possible since }m<m_n).
\]
Consider the line
\[
 L(x):=b_r+(m+\varepsilon)(x-r),\qquad x\in\mathbb R.
\]
We show that every point P_k, $k\neq r$, lies strictly below this line.

(a)  k>r.  Then
\[
  L(k)-b_k=(m+\varepsilon)(k-r)-(b_k-b_r)\ge(m+\varepsilon)(k-r)-m(k-r)=\varepsilon(k-r)>0.
\]

(b)  n<k<r.  From the definition of $m$ one has $b_k-b_n\le m(k-n)$, whence
\[
  b_k-b_r\le m(k-n)-m(r-n)=m(k-r).
\]
Therefore
\[
  L(k)-b_k\ge m(r-n)+(m+\varepsilon)(k-r)-m(k-n)=\varepsilon(k-r)>0.
\]

(c)  k\le n.  Since $\ell_n$ is supporting,
\[
 b_k<b_n+m_n(k-n). 
\]
Using $b_r=b_n+m(r-n)$ one obtains
\[
 \begin{aligned}
  L(k)-b_k&=b_r+(m+\varepsilon)(k-r)-b_k\\
          &=b_n+m(r-n)+(m+\varepsilon)(k-r)-b_k\\
          &=\bigl(b_n+m_n(k-n)-b_k\bigr)
             +\bigl(m-m_n+\varepsilon\bigr)(k-n).
 \end{aligned}
\]
The first bracket is positive, while $k-n\le0$ and 
$m-m_n+\varepsilon<-(m_n-m)+\varepsilon<0$, so the second bracket is non-negative.  Hence $L(k)-b_k>0$.

Thus every point P_k ($k\neq r$) lies strictly below L, so P_r is a vertex.

Step 3.  There are infinitely many vertices.
-------------------------------------------
Start with the already-established vertex $n_0:=0$ and repeatedly apply Step 2.  This yields a strictly increasing sequence of indices
\[
 0=n_0<n_1<n_2<\dotsm\longrightarrow\infty,
\]
showing that the set of vertices is infinite.

Step 4.  A vertex produces the required inequalities.
----------------------------------------------------
Let $n$ be any vertex (other than $0$) and let $y=b_n+m(x-n)$ be one of its supporting lines ($m>0$).  Because every other point of the form P_{n\pm j} ($1\le j\le n$) lies strictly below this line, we have
\[
  b_{n+j}<b_n+mj, \qquad b_{n-j}<b_n-mj\quad(1\le j\le n).
\]
Adding the two inequalities gives
\[
  b_{n-j}+b_{n+j}<2b_n\qquad(j=1,2,\dots ,n).
\]
Since, by Step 3, there are infinitely many such vertices $n$, the inequalities are satisfied for infinitely many positive integers $n$, as required.\hfill$\square$

core_steps: Embed the points (n, a_n) in the plane and study the upper convex hull S of these points.
Use lim (a_n / n) = 0 to show that the first point (1, a_1) is an exposed upper vertex (a supporting line exists with all other points below).
Given any upper vertex index n, take the largest k>n that maximizes the slope (a_kâˆ’a_n)/(kâˆ’n); its supporting line proves k is a new vertex, so the vertex set is infinite by induction.
For each vertex n, the supporting line inequalities a_{n+i} < a_n + mi and a_{nâˆ’i} < a_n âˆ’ mi (1â‰¤iâ‰¤nâˆ’1) add up to a_{nâˆ’i}+a_{n+i} < 2a_n, giving the required condition.

slot1.description: The requirement that a_n be positive can be relaxed to â€˜real-valuedâ€™; positivity is never used in the convex-hull or slope arguments.

slot2.description: â€˜Increasingâ€™ can be weakened to â€˜non-decreasingâ€™; the proof only needs slopes (a_kâˆ’a_n)/(kâˆ’n) to be â‰¥0.

slot3.description: The indexing could start at 0, 2, etc.; any fixed finite shift leaves the convex-hull argument unchanged.
